headline: AI Weekly: major new deals and stumped Fed officials
summary:
STORY: From major new deals to stumped Fed officials, 

This is AI Weekly. 

:: AI Weekly 

OpenAI is considering a contract with NATO, a source confirmed to Reuters. 

It comes days after the ChatGPT-owner struck a deal with the Pentagon. 

An initial Wall Street Journal report said that the contract opportunity was to deploy AI tools on NATO's "unclassified networks."

That’s in contrast with its Pentagon agreement, which will see its technology used in classified networks there. 

NATO didn't immediately respond to requests for comment. 

Amazon said Monday it would invest an additional $21 billion in Spain. 

That’s to expand its data centers and boost AI innovation.

It brings total investment in the country to $39 billion.

Amazon’s chief global officer said the investment would support up to 30,000 jobs until 2035. 

Taiwan’s January export orders hit a record, with strong demand for AI chips and technology. 

Home of the world’s largest contract chipmaker TSMC, orders from Taiwan are considered the bellwether of global technology demand.

January’s orders rose 60% from a year earlier, beating expectations. 

And the Ministry for Economic Affairs predicted ongoing momentum for orders as AI and high-performance computing continues to expand.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung met Singapore's Prime Minister Lawrence Wong Monday to discuss expanding cooperation in technology. 

The summit included talks on AI and nuclear energy. 

Lee and Wong announced the start of negotiations to upgrade the countries' existing free trade agreement.

And South Korea said the countries signed five pacts in fields including AI, nuclear power generation and space satellites. 

The U.S. Federal Reserve is racing to understand the pace with which AI will affect the economy. 

Officials have largely accepted the technology will lead to dramatic economic shifts.

But are seeing a divide emerging over its potential to impact the labor market and prices. 

This was highlighted by tech firm Block, which announced Thursday it would shed 40% of its workers - 4,000 people - due to AI-related developments. 

Rising layoffs would traditionally lean central bankers towards looser monetary policy.

But some say the AI transition may mean higher unemployment rates are par for the course.

content.title: AI Weekly: major new deals and stumped Fed officials
content.summary:
STORY: From major new deals to stumped Fed officials, 

This is AI Weekly. 

:: AI Weekly 

OpenAI is considering a contract with NATO, a source confirmed to Reuters. 

It comes days after the ChatGPT-owner struck a deal with the Pentagon. 

An initial Wall Street Journal report said that the contract opportunity was to deploy AI tools on NATO's "unclassified networks."

That’s in contrast with its Pentagon agreement, which will see its technology used in classified networks there. 

NATO didn't immediately respond to requests for comment. 

Amazon said Monday it would invest an additional $21 billion in Spain. 

That’s to expand its data centers and boost AI innovation.

It brings total investment in the country to $39 billion.

Amazon’s chief global officer said the investment would support up to 30,000 jobs until 2035. 

Taiwan’s January export orders hit a record, with strong demand for AI chips and technology. 

Home of the world’s largest contract chipmaker TSMC, orders from Taiwan are considered the bellwether of global technology demand.

January’s orders rose 60% from a year earlier, beating expectations. 

And the Ministry for Economic Affairs predicted ongoing momentum for orders as AI and high-performance computing continues to expand.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung met Singapore's Prime Minister Lawrence Wong Monday to discuss expanding cooperation in technology. 

The summit included talks on AI and nuclear energy. 

Lee and Wong announced the start of negotiations to upgrade the countries' existing free trade agreement.

And South Korea said the countries signed five pacts in fields including AI, nuclear power generation and space satellites. 

The U.S. Federal Reserve is racing to understand the pace with which AI will affect the economy. 

Officials have largely accepted the technology will lead to dramatic economic shifts.

But are seeing a divide emerging over its potential to impact the labor market and prices. 

This was highlighted by tech firm Block, which announced Thursday it would shed 40% of its workers - 4,000 people - due to AI-related developments. 

Rising layoffs would traditionally lean central bankers towards looser monetary policy.

But some say the AI transition may mean higher unemployment rates are par for the course.

headline: Soaring Power Prices Have US Politicians Searching for Solutions
summary: The Energy Department has loaned $26.5 billion — the most in its history — to help a big Southeastern utility reduce the cost of building new power plants in Georgia and Alabama.  Meanwhile, the nation’s biggest grid operator has proposed capping wholesale prices and some governors are weighing utility rate freezes as a way to keep a lid on electricity costs.  The affordability problem is prompting a White House meeting Wednesday with technology executives.

content.title: Soaring Power Prices Have US Politicians Searching for Solutions
content.summary: The Energy Department has loaned $26.5 billion — the most in its history — to help a big Southeastern utility reduce the cost of building new power plants in Georgia and Alabama.  Meanwhile, the nation’s biggest grid operator has proposed capping wholesale prices and some governors are weighing utility rate freezes as a way to keep a lid on electricity costs.  The affordability problem is prompting a White House meeting Wednesday with technology executives.

headline: Exclusive-Anthropic investors push to de-escalate Pentagon clash over AI safeguards, sources say
summary: Some Anthropic investors are racing to contain fallout from the AI research lab's dispute with the Pentagon, seven people familiar with the matter said, for fear that an ongoing spat could devastate the company's business.  In recent days, CEO Dario Amodei has discussed the matter with some of ‌Anthropic's major investors and partners, including Amazon.com CEO Andy Jassy, two of the people said.  Venture capital firms including Lightspeed and Iconiq have also been in contact with Anthropic executives, ‌two sources said.

headline: Exclusive-Big tech group supports Anthropic in Pentagon fight as investors push to de-escalate clash over AI safeguards
summary: A big tech industry group consisting of major Anthropic backers Amazon and Nvidia on Wednesday expressed concern over the Pentagon's decision to declare the artificial intelligence company a supply-chain risk as other investors raced to contain fallout from the lab's fight with the U.S. Defense Department.  In a letter dated Wednesday, the Information Technology ‌Industry Council, whose members include Nvidia, Amazon.com, Apple and OpenAI said "We are concerned by recent reports regarding the Department of War’s consideration of imposing a supply-chain risk designation in response to a procurement dispute."  The letter ‌does not name Anthropic.

content.title: Exclusive-Big tech group supports Anthropic in Pentagon fight as investors push to de-escalate clash over AI safeguards
content.summary: A big tech industry group consisting of major Anthropic backers Amazon and Nvidia on Wednesday expressed concern over the Pentagon's decision to declare the artificial intelligence company a supply-chain risk as other investors raced to contain fallout from the lab's fight with the U.S. Defense Department.  In a letter dated Wednesday, the Information Technology ‌Industry Council, whose members include Nvidia, Amazon.com, Apple and OpenAI said "We are concerned by recent reports regarding the Department of War’s consideration of imposing a supply-chain risk designation in response to a procurement dispute."  The letter ‌does not name Anthropic.

headline: Amazon cuts more jobs; this time in robotics unit
summary: SAN FRANCISCO, March 4 (Reuters) - Amazon on Tuesday confirmed it laid off staff across its robotics unit, ‌with at least 100 white-collar jobs affected, two people ‌familiar with the matter told Reuters. This comes after a January cut of about ​16,000 jobs with the company at the time hinting layoffs would continue.

headline: Broadcom forecasts second-quarter revenue above estimates
summary: Chip designer Broadcom forecast second-quarter revenue above Wall Street estimates on ‌Wednesday, a sign of strong demand for ‌advanced chips used in data centers powering artificial intelligence applications.  The ​company expects quarterly revenue to be about $22.0 billion, above analysts' average estimate of $20.56 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.  "Our AI revenue growth is ‌accelerating, and we ⁠expect AI semiconductor revenue to be $10.7 billion in Q2," CEO Hock Tan said ⁠in a statement.

headline: Whoop to Expand Staff by 75% to Spur Growth Ahead of Likely IPO
summary: The Boston-based company intends to add more than 600 new roles across various departments, including software, research, design, hardware, product development, manufacturing, sales and marketing, founder and Chief Executive Office Will Ahmed said in an interview announcing the expansion.  The majority of the new employees will be based at the company’s headquarters, he said, with additional hiring planned across North America, Europe, Asia and Gulf Cooperation Council nations, including Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.

headline: Big Tech signs Trump's AI energy cost pledge
summary: President Trump announced a new "ratepayer protection pledge," saying energy demand tied to AI data centers could more than triple by 2035, promising American households won't see higher electricity bills as a result. Yahoo Finance Senior Reporter Jennifer Schonberger joins Yahoo Finance host Josh Lipton to discuss the big tech companies under the agreement, Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), OpenAI (OPAI.PVT), Amazon (AMZN), and xAI, and their pledge to fully fund new power generation and infrastructure needed for their AI facilities. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination.

headline: Qualcomm Targets Robotics And AI Native 6G To Broaden Growth Story
summary: Qualcomm announced a push into robotics with its new Dragonwing processor, built specifically for robotics platforms. The company also revealed a coalition with Amazon, Google, and Microsoft to develop AI native 6G, aiming for device demonstrations by 2028 and global rollout from 2029. These moves are intended to broaden Qualcomm’s business beyond smartphones into next generation connectivity and AI infrastructure. QUALCOMM (NasdaqGS:QCOM) is making this shift while its share price trades...

headline: Broadcom sees revenue above estimates as AI fuels custom chip demand
summary: Chip designer Broadcom forecast second-quarter revenue above Wall Street estimates on Wednesday, a sign of strong demand for advanced chips used in data centers ‌powering artificial intelligence applications.  Big Tech firms such as Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta are expected to spend at least $630 billion ​to build AI infrastructure this year, which would boost demand for chips, servers, storage and networking equipment from companies like Broadcom.  "Our AI revenue growth is accelerating, and we expect AI semiconductor revenue to be $10.7 billion in Q2," CEO Hock Tan said in a statement.

content.title: Broadcom sees revenue above estimates as AI fuels custom chip demand
content.summary: Chip designer Broadcom forecast second-quarter revenue above Wall Street estimates on Wednesday, a sign of strong demand for advanced chips used in data centers ‌powering artificial intelligence applications.  Big Tech firms such as Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta are expected to spend at least $630 billion ​to build AI infrastructure this year, which would boost demand for chips, servers, storage and networking equipment from companies like Broadcom.  "Our AI revenue growth is accelerating, and we expect AI semiconductor revenue to be $10.7 billion in Q2," CEO Hock Tan said in a statement.

headline: Broadcom sees over $100 billion in AI chip sales by 2027 on robust custom chip demand
summary: Chip designer Broadcom on Wednesday projected its artificial intelligence chip revenue would exceed $100 billion next year, signaling surging demand for custom chips in a market dominated by Nvidia.  Big Tech firms such as ‌Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta are expected to spend at least $630 billion to build AI infrastructure this year, boosting demand for chips, servers, storage and networking equipment from companies like Broadcom.  Broadcom, which supplies semiconductors and infrastructure software, does not typically design entire AI chips itself.

content.title: Broadcom sees over $100 billion in AI chip sales by 2027 on robust custom chip demand
content.summary: Chip designer Broadcom on Wednesday projected its artificial intelligence chip revenue would exceed $100 billion next year, signaling surging demand for custom chips in a market dominated by Nvidia.  Big Tech firms such as ‌Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta are expected to spend at least $630 billion to build AI infrastructure this year, boosting demand for chips, servers, storage and networking equipment from companies like Broadcom.  Broadcom, which supplies semiconductors and infrastructure software, does not typically design entire AI chips itself.
